nativeRange.habitat: Calm to moderately-flowing headwaters and minor tributaries; substrates of sand, gravel, rocks, and boulders with leaf litter, submerged driftwood, and aquatic vegetation

diet.notes: Micropredator in the wild, feeding on small invertebrates and zooplankton. In captivity, generally accepts high-quality micro foods such as finely crushed flakes, micro pellets (under 0.5 mm), and powdered foods as a staple. Frozen or live daphnia, baby brine shrimp, cyclops, micro worms, and rotifers are well-regarded supplements. Food must be very small to fit their tiny mouths.

summary: Tiny iridescent nano fish from Thailand and Myanmar, reaching only ~2 cm. Displays a radioactive yellow-green lateral stripe. Best kept in schools of 8+ in soft, slightly acidic planted tanks; pairs well with shrimp.
careNotes:
One of the smallest aquarium fish, reaching approximately 1.5–2 cm. Native to well-oxygenated headwater streams in northwestern peninsular Thailand (Ranong and Phang Nga provinces, Kanchanaburi and Tak provinces) and the Ataran River basin in southern Myanmar. Wild specimens are reported to occur in groups of 20–50 individuals, so a school of at least 8 is generally recommended for confident behavior in captivity.

The characteristic iridescent yellow-green lateral stripe is brightest against a dark substrate and background. This species is sometimes confused with Microrasbora erythromicron (Emerald Dwarf Rasbora, now classified in the genus Danio), which has a different banding pattern and originates from Lake Inle in Myanmar. Microdevario kubotai belongs to a different genus (Microdevario) from the typical Boraras nanofish, though care requirements overlap significantly.

Taxonomy note: FishBase currently classifies this species under family Danionidae (a split from the broader Cyprinidae). Most aquarium hobby references, including SeriouslyFish, retain the Cyprinidae classification; Cyprinidae is used here for consistency with hobby usage.

Water quality: Sources consistently note this species does best in biologically mature, well-established tanks with stable parameters. Sensitivity to elevated nitrates and sudden parameter changes is commonly reported; weekly water changes of 30–50% are generally advised.

Tank size: SeriouslyFish recommends a minimum 60 × 30 cm footprint (~54 L) for a school. A 40 L tank with a 45 cm footprint can work as a minimum for small schools in nano setups, but a larger tank is generally advised for groups of 10 or more.

Care level: User-supplied data indicated beginner, but SeriouslyFish and Fish Laboratory both rate this species as intermediate due to sensitivity to water quality and stress. Intermediate is used here as the more representative rating from primary sources.

This species will not bother adult-sized dwarf shrimp and is widely recommended as a shrimp-safe tankmate. They may opportunistically consume shrimp fry or very small juvenile shrimp.
breedingNotes: Moderate difficulty. Egg-scatterers with no parental care — adults tend to consume eggs. Spawning generally occurs among fine-leaved aquatic plants or Java moss. Incubation has been reported to take approximately 72 hours; exact timing is temperature-dependent. A dedicated breeding tank with a fine mesh floor or thick moss layer is generally advised to protect eggs from predation. Fry are very small and require infusoria or similar microscopic foods initially, graduating to baby brine shrimp nauplii and micro worms after a week or more. Breeding in captivity occurs fairly readily when pairs are in good condition and water quality is stable.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish
primary.notes: Primary reference for taxonomy, native range, water parameters, tank requirements, and husbandry. Used as primary due to depth of scientific and care detail.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op
additional.notes: Cross-check for care level, diet, group size (8–12), tank size (5-gallon minimum per article), temperament, and tankmate suggestions. Temperature listed as 72–80°F (22–27°C); pH 6–7 confirmed. No dedicated species page found; data drawn from Top 10 Rasboras article.

additional.name: Fish Laboratory
additional.notes: Cross-check for water parameters (20–27°C, pH 6.0–7.0 confirmed), adult size (1.5–2 cm confirmed), lifespan (3–7 years), minimum tank (54 L / 15 gal), care level (intermediate to advanced noted), and breeding difficulty (moderate confirmed).

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Taxonomy cross-check: family listed as Danionidae (modern reclassification) vs Cyprinidae used by SeriouslyFish and aquarium hobby sources. Adult size confirmed at ~1.9 cm SL.

fish.breedingNotes: Egg-scatterer; no parental care; adults eat eggs. Breeding tank with fine mesh or Java moss recommended. Fry require microscopic foods initially.
